one_line_takeaway: The first survey to unify hardware-level (side-channel, fault-injection, Trojan), software-level (adversarial, backdoor, inference), and primitive-level (PUF/TRNG) security research on neuromorphic/spiking-neural-network systems into one empirically-derived taxonomy built from 133 papers filtered out of 807 candidates.
key_finding: The survey's central empirical finding is a quantified imbalance and set of gaps in the literature it collected: hardware attacks (50 papers) and software attacks (43 papers) are numerically comparable, but coverage is uneven within categories -- e.g., hardware Trojans (6 papers) are far less studied than side-channel (25) or fault-injection (19) attacks on neuromorphic hardware, and membership inference (3 papers) is far less studied than adversarial attacks (20) on the software side. The survey also identifies specific structural gaps rather than just counts: (1) no prior survey unifies hardware, software, and PUF/TRNG-level neuromorphic security under one framework (contrasted explicitly against Horvath et al. [47], which covers side-channel attack phases on conventional platforms but excludes SNNs, and Wang et al. [137], which covers IMC power side-channels but not broader hardware defenses or cryptographic primitives); (2) transistor-aging/retention-drift side channels have not been studied specifically within neuromorphic memory arrays; (3) hardware-Trojan detection benchmarks/datasets for neuromorphic platforms are lacking despite several recent Trojan demonstrations (Meyers et al., Wu et al., Raptis et al.); and (4) publication volume in neuromorphic security has grown steadily from 2014 to 2025 (Figure 6), signaling an immature but rapidly expanding field.
core_contribution: A systematic literature survey (not an original attack/defense proposal) that maps the security landscape of neuromorphic computing systems, unifying hardware-level threats (side-channel attacks, fault injection, hardware Trojans), software-level threats (adversarial, backdoor, membership-inference and model-inversion attacks on spiking neural networks), and security-enabling hardware primitives (PUFs, TRNGs) into a single taxonomy. The authors derive this taxonomy empirically from a structured literature search (initial pool of 807 papers across IEEE Xplore, ACM, Nature, Elsevier, Springer, Wiley, IACR, and ArXiv, filtered down to 133 papers spanning Jan 2014-Aug 2025) rather than adopting a pre-existing framework, and organize the 133 papers into three top-level categories: Hardware Attacks (50 papers: 25 SCA, 19 FIA, 6 Hardware Trojan), Software Attacks (43 papers: 20 adversarial, 12 software SCA/FIA, 8 backdoor, 3 membership inference), and Applications in Security (40 papers: 19 PUF, 11 TRNG, 10 security features). The paper also contributes a maintained open-source repository of curated references and comparison tables, and formulates six explicit research questions (RQ1-RQ6) that structure the review's scope (trends/gaps, hardware attacks, software attacks, countermeasures, PUF/TRNG integration, and deployment environments).
threat_model: As a survey, the paper does not define a single threat model of its own; instead it catalogs the threat models used across the reviewed literature and organizes them along axes captured in its own taxonomy (Figure 4): attacker capability (white-box/gray-box/black-box), interaction mode (passive observation vs. active fault injection), physical proximity (remote vs. local), and profiling status (profiled vs. non-profiled). It surveys attacks targeting memristor crossbar arrays, SNN models, FPGA/ASIC/SoC implementations, microcontrollers/edge devices, neuromorphic chips/accelerators, and PUF/TRNG circuits, exploiting properties such as power consumption, EM emanations, timing, memory access patterns, and input perturbability, with attacker aims spanning data theft, model stealing, model manipulation, and access-control bypass.
limitations: As a survey, the paper's evidentiary basis is secondary -- claims about attack effectiveness (e.g., MERCURY's <1% topology-recovery error, NeuroHammer's 70% bit-flip rate in <10,000 access cycles, CPA needing as few as 50 traces) are reported from the cited primary studies, not independently reproduced or validated by the authors. The authors themselves flag that several underlying results rest on simulation rather than physical hardware (e.g., they explicitly caveat that NeuroHammer's simulated bit-flip results 'should be interpreted as indicative rather than conclusive evidence' given dependence on device geometry, material properties, and thermal coupling). The search methodology, while structured, is limited to 8 named digital libraries and a fixed keyword taxonomy that could miss relevant grey literature or differently-termed work, and paper selection/categorization was done manually, introducing potential classifier subjectivity despite the stated inclusion/exclusion process.

datasets_used: Literature corpus: 807 papers retrieved across IEEE Xplore (330), ACM (138), Nature (92), Elsevier (74), Springer (59), Wiley (52), IACR (46), ArXiv (16), filtered to 133 papers (2014-2025) for the survey's own taxonomy
MNIST and FashionMNIST (cited as datasets used by surveyed attack papers, e.g., Isik et al.'s FPGA power side-channel study, and the SOT-MTJ bit-flip fault paper)
N-MNIST and IBM DVS128 Gesture, plus static MNIST and AT&T Face (cited as datasets used in the surveyed BrainLeaks model-inversion study)
CIFAR-10, CIFAR-100, ImageNet (cited as datasets used in the surveyed Moshruba et al. membership-inference comparison of SNNs vs. ANNs)
quantitative_results: Literature funnel: 807 raw records -> 133 papers after de-duplication and screening (survey's own methodology, Figure 5)

Taxonomy counts (survey's own classification, Table 3): Hardware Attacks 50 total (SCA 25, FIA 19, Hardware Trojans 6); Software Attacks 43 total (Adversarial 20, Software SCA/FIA 12, Backdoor 8, Membership Inference 3); Applications in Security 40 total (PUF 19, TRNG 11, Security Features 10)

Cited (not the survey's own) numeric results reported from surveyed primary papers: MERCURY framework recovers victim model topology with <1% error; NeuroHammer achieves ~70% bit-flip rate in targeted cells with fewer than 10,000 access cycles; a CPA attack on a stream cipher used as few as 50 power traces; a checksum-based fault-tolerant IMC architecture achieves over 90% recovery of model accuracy; MRAM/RRAM/PCM/SRAM attacks reported with trace counts ranging from ~100 mV power intervals, >300K data points per trace, to results 'observed across 13 devices'
baselines_compared: The survey positions itself against two prior related works rather than against a numeric baseline: Horvath et al. [47] (an SoK on side-channel attack phases/threat models for conventional computing platforms, explicitly noted as not including SNNs) and Wang et al. [137] (a study of side-channel-driven model extraction from in-memory-computing power traces, noted as not exploring broader hardware defenses or cryptographic primitives). Table 1 in the paper also compares against a general neuromorphic-computing survey [119] and a HW/SW security survey [128] on scope and main findings.
